text: The video shows a small uterus displaced anteriorly by a large cervical fibroid, visibly stretching the round ligament.
The surgeon begins by dissecting the uterovascular fold and carefully pushing the bladder down, creating space for further surgical access.
The surgeon pushes the bladder down to the level of the urethral meatus, exposing the surgical field for further dissection.
The surgeon opens the retroperitoneum on both sides, revealing underlying anatomical structures and preparing for further dissection during the laparoscopic myomectomy.
The surgeon opens the retroperitoneum on the left side, revealing underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon isolates the uterine artery while carefully lateralizing the ureter to prevent injury during the laparoscopic myomectomy procedure.
The uterine artery is dissected, and the ureter is clearly visible in the surgical field, demonstrating successful isolation and exposure of these structures.

The surgeon places a ligating clip over the anterior division of the internal iliac artery, which has been dissected above by tracing the obliterated umbilical artery, due to the presence of a central cervical fibroid.
The internal iliac artery is ligated on one side, following the dissection and exposure of the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon repeats the ligation of the internal iliac artery on the opposite side, mirroring the steps previously performed on the initial side.
The surgeon demonstrates the ureter, which is visibly free and has been lateralized away from the uterine artery.
The surgeon carefully moves the ureter laterally, away from the ureteric tunnel, ensuring the vaginal region is isolated and free from the fibroid.
The surgeon isolates and lateralizes the ureter on the opposite side, lifting it up to dissect the anterior division of the internal iliac artery.
The surgeon clips the anterior division of the internal iliac artery after dissection to prevent blood loss during the laparoscopic myomectomy procedure.
The surgical field is prepared for the laparoscopic myomectomy, with the ureters lateralized and the anterior division of the internal iliac artery clipped to minimize blood loss.
The surgeon makes an incision along the vaginal wall, preparing the area for further surgical steps in the myomectomy procedure.
The surgeon performs a horizontal incision over the anterior vaginal wall to address a fibroid extending towards the caudal region.
The fibroid is visible, accompanied by a noticeable blood spill in the surgical field.
The surgeon separates the entire fibroid from the anterior vaginal wall, dissecting it upwards to remove it from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses a myoma screw to apply gentle traction and counter traction, lifting the fibroid off its base. The fibroid is visibly dissected away from its adherence to the cervix.
The base of the fibroid is visible, showing adherence to the cervix.
After completing the dissection, the surgeon prepares to suture the anterior vaginal wall where the horizontal incision was made.
The opened vaginal wall is visible, with the cervix dilated and a fibroid arising from the cervical canal at the center of the surgical field.
The anterior lip of the cervix is visible, providing a clear view of the cervical anatomy during the procedure.
The surgeon uses quilt sutures to completely close the anterior vaginal wall. The fibroid is then removed with a disposable morcellator blade, visibly separating it from the surrounding tissue.
The uterus is shown regaining its normal size at the conclusion of the surgery.
